metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = bound state / large scattering length / resonance peak, CRIMSON = unbound / small scattering length / off-resonance
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no partial-wave expansion, no Levinson theorem statement, no three-body Efimov states, no Feshbach-resonance Hamiltonian formalism

beats.narration_text: An ultracold cesium gas. A researcher sweeps a magnetic field. At one precise field value, the scattering cross-section spikes by a factor of ten thousand.
A few milligauss away, the cross-section collapses back. Right at that field, a new two-body bound state has flickered into existence at zero binding energy.
Well depth changing smoothly should change cross-sections smoothly. Yet adding one barely-bound state sends the cross-section to infinity. Why?

props.headline: Well depth changing smoothly should change cross-sections smoothly. Yet adding one…

card.headline: One new bound state. Cross-section: infinity.
card.dek: Why does a barely-bound state at threshold send the cross-section to infinity?

beats.narration_text: At low energy, the scattering cross-section depends on one number: the scattering length. A bigger scattering length means a bigger effective target area.
The cross-section is four pi times the scattering length squared. Normally the scattering length is just a few nanometers — a reasonable number.
But when a bound state exists at exactly zero energy — exactly at threshold — the wave function barely decays outside the well. The decay length becomes enormous.

props.headline: But when a bound state exists at exactly zero energy —…

beats.narration_text: Here is a potential well, and its bound state. As the binding energy shrinks toward zero, the wave function leaks further and further outside the well.
At exactly zero binding energy, the wave function never fully decays. It extends to infinity outside. The decay length, which is also the scattering length, diverges.
Plot the scattering length against well depth. Each time a new bound state appears at threshold, the scattering length passes through plus and minus infinity — a sign flip and a divergence.
Since the cross-section goes as scattering length squared, it diverges as infinity squared — infinity. The particle sees an infinitely large target because the bound state has no preferred size.

props.headline: Since the cross-section goes as scattering length squared, it diverges as…

card.headline: sigma ~ 4 pi * a^2  -->  infinity
card.dek: Scattering length diverges at threshold. Cross-section diverges with it.

beats.narration_text: A Feshbach resonance uses a magnetic field to tune the bound-state energy across threshold. That gives experimenters a dial to set the scattering length — from near zero to plus or minus infinity.
Turn the scattering length to infinity and the gas forms a Bose-Einstein condensate with the strongest possible interactions. Set it to zero and the gas becomes nearly ideal.

Illustrative: a spherical well of depth 0.3 electron volts and radius one nanometer has no bound state — cross-section 0.08 square nanometers. Increase depth to 0.38 electron volts: the first bound state appears at threshold, scattering length diverges to a thousand nanometers, cross-section spikes to 10 million square nanometers. Push depth to 0.42 electron volts: bound state now 0.01 electron volts below threshold, scattering length drops to 12 nanometers, cross-section 1800 square nanometers.
A barely-bound state sends the cross-section to infinity because the scattering length diverges at threshold. Quantum mechanics.

props.headline: A barely-bound state sends the cross-section to infinity because the scattering…

card.headline: Threshold bound state: scattering length diverges.
card.dek: Cross-section scales as a-squared — so it diverges too.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems series.

props.seriesTitle: Quantum Mechanics: Approximation Methods and Real Systems
